text: We present a case of a fifty-five-year-old female who had a posterior repair in the past and presented with a recurrent rectocele. She also had symptoms of obstructive defecation with evidence of recto-rectal interception and internal prolapse.

The patient is positioned in the modified Lloyd-Davis position on an anti-slip mattress. The surgical setup includes an optical 10mm port at the umbilicus, with two additional ports on the right side and one on the left side.
The surgeon begins the procedure by placing a trans-abdominal stitch using a straight Prolene suture to elevate the uterus and open the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory using monopolar diathermy on scissors, with the line of the right ureter visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision, allowing CO₂ insufflation to dissect the tissue planes. A clearly defined plane is developed behind the mesorectal package, with careful identification and preservation of the hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon performs dissection in a craniocaudal direction along the right border of the rectum, carefully separating tissue planes in this area.

The surgeon extends the incision to open the rectovaginal septum, carefully identifying the back wall of the vagina. Proper traction is applied to the rectum to open the peritoneal fold, ensuring the avascular plane is entered without damaging the rectum or vagina.

The surgeon uses their left hand to control and manipulate the rectum from right to left, cutting through scar tissue in the retropaginal septum without placing retractors in the vagina. Proper traction is applied to ensure cutting in the correct plane due to tissue laxity in patients with prolapse.
The surgeon continues dissection down to the level of the perineal body, using a year-end's crasper on the rectum to assess the extent of dissection and perform a detailed rectal examination.

The surgeon performs precise dissection using monopolar diathermy, carefully avoiding thermal damage to the rectal and vaginal walls. The extent of dissection is evaluated, reaching approximately three to four centimeters from the anal verge, at the top of the anal canal.

The surgeon uses an IPOM polypropylene mesh with a protective covering on one side, measuring fifteen by five centimeters. The mesh is secured to the anterior rectal wall using four Ethicon IIO sutures.

The surgeon carefully places sutures on the muscular area of the rectum to secure the mesh, ensuring that the sutures do not penetrate the full thickness of the rectal wall to minimize infection risk. The stitching is performed intracorporeally in a narrow space.

The surgeon places four sutures approximately one centimeter apart to secure the mesh to the anterior rectal wall. A 30-degree camera provides a clear view deep in the pelvis, facilitating precise suturing. Two needle holders are used to ensure the sutures are tight and avoid leaving any loose ends.
The surgeon secures the proximal end of the mesh to the sacral promontory using tackers, ensuring to avoid tacking near the inferior mesenteric artery.

Judgment of tension on the mesh is very subjective. Mesh should not be lax to allow the prolapse to happen again. However, if it is fixed in to taut, it can open up the anorectal angle creating problems with continence.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal fold over the mesh using a running suture of two OPDS, ensuring the mesh is completely covered and positioned extraperitoneally.
